title: ARIMA: Tying it All Together
concept: ARIMA combines Autoregression (AR), Differencing (I), and Moving Average (MA) using three parameters (p, d, q) to forecast complex time series.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the purpose of the 'Integration' (I) part of a time series model?

options.text: To make the data stationary by differencing consecutive points to remove trends
To combine predictions from multiple different unsupervised clusters
To calculate the moving average of the errors

exercises.explain: Perfect! 'I' stands for Integration, which differences the data to make it stationary and then integrates it back. Now, let's meet the combined champion!

cards.title: The Three-in-One Tool
cards.body: If you want to build a table, you don't just use a hammer, a saw, or a measuring tape. You need all three. Similarly, real-world time series have trends, past patterns, and sudden shocks all at once. ARIMA is the ultimate toolbox that joins AR, I, and MA into a single super-model.

cards.title: What is ARIMA?
cards.body: ARIMA stands for AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average. It is a unified model that combines three parts: the AR part (past values), the I part (differencing for stationarity), and the MA part (past errors).

cards.title: The Recipe of ARIMA (p, d, q)
cards.body:
Think of ARIMA as a recipe with three ingredients, written as (p, d, q):
• p (AR): How many past steps (lags) to look at.
• d (I): How many times to difference the data to make it stationary.
• q (MA): How many past errors (shocks) to look at.

cards.title: ARIMA(1, 1, 1) in Action
cards.body:
Suppose we configure an ARIMA model with (p=1, d=1, q=1). This means the model will:
1. Difference the data once (d=1) to remove trends.
2. Look back at 1 past value (p=1).
3. Look back at 1 past error (q=1) to adjust for sudden shocks.

cards.title: Parameters Aren't Random Guesses
cards.body: You might think data scientists just guess the (p, d, q) values randomly. In reality, they look at special correlation charts of the past data (called ACF and PACF plots) to see how long past patterns and shocks linger, letting them pick the perfect recipe.

cards.title: The Anatomy of ARIMA(p, d, q)
cards.body: Understanding the three tuning dials of an ARIMA model:

left.text:
p (AR order): Lags of the target variable.
d (I order): Number of differencing steps (usually 0, 1, or 2).

right.text:
q (MA order): Lags of the forecast errors.
Allows the model to smooth out short-term random shocks.

options.text: Automated Regression for Image Model Analysis

exercises.explain: Correct! ARIMA stands for AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average, combining all three concepts.

exercises.prompt: True or False: In ARIMA(p, d, q), the parameter 'd' represents how many past prediction errors the model looks at.
exercises.explain: False. The parameter 'd' represents the degree of differencing (the 'I' part), while 'q' represents the number of past prediction errors (the 'MA' part).

exercises.sentence: In the ARIMA(p, d, q) model, the parameter that controls the Autoregression (AR) lookback is ___.
exercises.explain: The parameter 'p' specifies the number of autoregressive lags (past values) used in the model.

exercises.prompt: If you set up ARIMA(2, 0, 1), how will the model behave?

options.text: It will look back at 2 past values, apply 0 differencing, and look back at 1 past error
It will difference the data 2 times, look back at 0 past values, and look back at 1 past error
It will look back at 2 past errors, difference 0 times, and use 1 past value

exercises.explain: Yes! ARIMA(p, d, q) maps directly to p=2 (AR), d=0 (I), q=1 (MA).

exercises.prompt: You are explaining ARIMA to a colleague. They ask: 'If my sales data is already stable and has no overall trend, but has short-term shocks and depends heavily on yesterday's sales, what ARIMA(p, d, q) configuration makes sense?'

options.text: ARIMA(1, 0, 1) — because you need 1 lag of past values (p=1), no differencing since there is no trend (d=0), and 1 lag of errors to handle shocks (q=1)
ARIMA(0, 1, 0) — because you only need to difference the trend
ARIMA(1, 1, 1) — because you must always difference the data at least once
ARIMA(0, 0, 0) — because stable data requires no parameters

exercises.explain: Fabulous! Since the data is already stationary, we set d=0. We look back 1 step (p=1) and use 1 error correction lag (q=1) for shocks. Now, what about seasonal cycles like summer peaks? Let's check SARIMA!
